name: Meta-Learning Zoo
summary: Specialized agents coordinate through a shared forum to build composable ML models autonomously.
description: A multi-agent system where specialized Claude instances work together autonomously on a long-running research project. Each agent has a specific role (builder, verifier, strategist, librarian, debugger) and they coordinate through a shared forum file. The orchestrator spawns and monitors all agents. Forum posts can be upvoted/downvoted to surface consensus. On-demand agents (verifier, debugger) are spawned only when needed, reducing waste.
realWorldExample: Google DeepMind's AlphaFold team — specialized sub-groups for protein structure prediction, training, and verification, coordinating through shared experiment logs.
whenToUse: Long-running research or engineering projects requiring diverse expertise with continuous autonomous operation and minimal human oversight.
weaknesses: Forum can get noisy without cleanup
Orchestrator is single point of failure

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: Spawns, monitors, and coordinates all other agents

agents.name: Builder
agents.description: Writes code, trains models, implements features

agents.name: Verifier
agents.description: Independently verifies claims by loading checkpoints and running tests

agents.name: Strategist
agents.description: Sets scientific direction and makes architecture decisions

agents.name: Librarian
agents.description: Catalogs results, maintains documentation, cleans forum

agents.name: Debugger
agents.description: Deep debugging and root cause analysis on demand

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared forum for coordination, private agent instructions, persistent results tracked in files and JSON

diagram:
graph TD
    O[Orchestrator] --> B[Builder]
    O --> V[Verifier]
    O --> S[Strategist]
    O --> L[Librarian]
    O --> D[Debugger]
    B -.->|posts| F((FORUM.md))
    V -.->|posts| F
    S -.->|posts| F
    L -.->|cleans| F
    D -.->|posts| F

name: Military Chain of Command
summary: Strict tree hierarchy where orders flow down and situation reports flow up through fixed layers.
description: The classic military organizational model with strict hierarchy. Information and orders flow through a well-defined chain. Each agent reports to exactly one superior and commands one or more subordinates. No level may be skipped in communication. Designed for clarity of authority and accountability in high-stakes, time-critical environments where ambiguity can be fatal.
realWorldExample: US Army's 82nd Airborne Division — brigade commanders report to the division general, battalion commanders to brigade, company to battalion, all the way down to four-soldier fire teams.
whenToUse: When you need absolute clarity of authority, strict accountability, and disciplined execution of complex multi-phase plans under pressure.
weaknesses: Single point of failure at each node

agents.name: Commander
agents.description: Sets strategy, makes final decisions

agents.name: Field Commander
agents.description: Translates strategy into operational plans

agents.name: Unit Leader
agents.description: Leads tactical execution of assigned objectives

agents.name: Team Lead
agents.description: Directly supervises execution teams

agents.name: Operator
agents.description: Executes specific assigned tasks

memoryArchitecture.description: Strictly compartmentalized. Each level sees only what its clearance allows. Intelligence flows up, orders flow down.

diagram:
graph TD
    A[Commander] --> B1[Field Cmd 1]
    A --> B2[Field Cmd 2]
    B1 --> C1[Unit Leader 1]
    B1 --> C2[Unit Leader 2]
    B2 --> C3[Unit Leader 3]
    B2 --> C4[Unit Leader 4]
    C1 --> D1[Team Lead]
    C1 --> D2[Team Lead]
    C2 --> D3[Team Lead]
    C2 --> D4[Team Lead]

name: Holacracy
summary: Self-organizing circles with distributed authority where roles are fluid and governance is consent-based.
description: A system of nested, self-organizing circles where authority is distributed to roles rather than people. Each circle has a lead link (appointed by parent circle) and a rep link (elected by members) creating bidirectional information flow. Governance meetings use an integrative decision-making process where proposals pass unless there is a reasoned objection. Tensions drive evolution: anyone who senses a gap between current reality and potential can propose a change.
realWorldExample: Zappos adopted Holacracy company-wide in 2013, replacing all traditional managers with self-organizing circles — the experiment lasted until 2020 and became a landmark case study in whether flat structures scale.
whenToUse: When you need rapid adaptation to changing conditions, want to distribute decision-making authority, and can tolerate the learning curve of a novel governance system.

agents.name: Lead Link
agents.description: Assigns roles within circle, sets priorities, allocates resources

agents.name: Rep Link
agents.description: Elected representative carrying concerns to parent circle

agents.name: Facilitator
agents.description: Runs governance and tactical meetings using the process

agents.name: Secretary
agents.description: Records outputs of governance meetings, maintains records

agents.name: Circle Member
agents.description: Fills one or more roles, processes tensions

memoryArchitecture.description: Governance records are fully transparent. Roles and accountabilities are documented. Individual work context is private.

diagram:
graph TD
    GC((General Circle)) --> C1((Product Circle))
    GC --> C2((Engineering Circle))
    GC --> C3((Operations Circle))
    C1 --> C1a((Feature Team))
    C1 --> C1b((UX Team))
    C2 --> C2a((Backend))
    C2 --> C2b((Frontend))
    C1 -.->|rep link| GC
    C2 -.->|rep link| GC

name: Ant Colony
summary: Decentralized swarm where agents communicate indirectly through environmental signals, producing emergent intelligence.
description: Inspired by ant colonies where no central coordinator exists. Agents communicate indirectly through stigmergy: modifying the shared environment (leaving 'pheromone trails') which other agents sense and respond to. Complex collective behavior emerges from simple individual rules. Agents are individually simple but collectively solve problems like pathfinding, resource allocation, and task assignment through positive feedback loops.
whenToUse: When tasks can be decomposed into many simple parallel operations, when robustness to individual agent failure is critical, and when optimal solutions can emerge from local interactions.
weaknesses: Can get stuck in local optima

agents.name: Scout
agents.description: Explores environment randomly, leaves weak trails on discoveries

agents.name: Worker
agents.description: Follows strongest pheromone trails, reinforces successful paths

agents.name: Queen
agents.description: Spawns new agents based on colony needs (not a commander)

memoryArchitecture.description: Shared memory IS the environment. Pheromone signals decay over time creating natural forgetting. No centralized memory store.

diagram:
graph TD
    Q[Queen] -.->|spawns| S1[Scout]
    Q -.->|spawns| S2[Scout]
    Q -.->|spawns| W1[Worker]
    Q -.->|spawns| W2[Worker]
    Q -.->|spawns| W3[Worker]
    S1 -->|marks| E((Environment))
    S2 -->|marks| E
    W1 -->|reads & marks| E
    W2 -->|reads & marks| E
    W3 -->|reads & marks| E

name: Jazz Ensemble
summary: Small group of peers improvising together, each leading and following fluidly based on musical context.
description: Modeled after a jazz combo where leadership is fluid and emergent. Each agent is a skilled specialist who can both lead (solo) and support (comp). There is no fixed conductor; instead, agents listen to each other and respond in real-time. One agent may take the lead for a section while others adapt, then hand off leadership naturally. The shared 'chord chart' provides loose structure while allowing extensive improvisation within it.
realWorldExample: Miles Davis Quintet, jazz jam sessions
whenToUse: When the task requires creative problem-solving, agents are highly skilled and autonomous, and the output benefits from dynamic interplay and emergent solutions.

agents.name: Rhythm Section
agents.description: Provides foundational structure (bass, drums, piano comping)

agents.name: Lead Voice A
agents.description: Primary melodic agent, takes extended solos

agents.name: Lead Voice B
agents.description: Secondary melodic agent, trades phrases with A

agents.name: Harmonist
agents.description: Enriches texture, fills gaps, connects ideas

memoryArchitecture.description: Minimal shared structure (chord charts). Each agent maintains rich private context. Real-time shared state is the 'sound' itself.

diagram:
graph LR
    R[Rhythm Section] <--> S1[Lead Voice A]
    R <--> S2[Lead Voice B]
    R <--> H[Harmonist]
    S1 <--> S2
    S1 <--> H
    S2 <--> H
